content:
It was never a small task or an easy feat for Vornakir to admit when he was wrong, or when he made a mistake. It was near impossible for him for him to conceive he’s not completely and entirely on the right path always. 

Today was no different. At least, not in a conventional way. He didn’t think this was a mistake, he didn’t think this was something that painted him in the wrong. He fully intended to die, last night. He fully intended to sacrifice his life for someone else, to set aside his pride for once and just be a decent person. It would have been a mistake to let Zašia take the attack. It would have been wrong to not intervene with the plans the gods had and prove to her that he meant what he said. That his pride may be important to him but it didn’t always come first.

So why then was the taste of Death’s Sweet Tea — the finest wine he’d let wet his tongue and a late night kiss that never left his mind — he had experienced that previously washed down the taste of his own blood and disintegrated ash now replaced by shame and sourness? Why did guilt flood him? For but a fleeting moment in the silence that hung in his room that afternoon, it was because deep down he felt that he may become a burden. Sure he could still very efficiently cast his spells, they didn’t require obtuse somatic components or weaving. Sure, in a duel it wouldn’t drag him down - in fact it was statistically less surface area to take an attack and a thinner silhouette. 

But a man who feels shame at his core for the magic he’s so very often praised for, that he’s called a prodigy for, that he swore couldn’t possibly define him but only bolstered what was already there would become…a life line. Anything that required two hands to do for the rest of his life, he’d have to rely on a conjured mage hand. Sometimes he’d need help sitting up in bed if he was especially sore where normally he could use his own hands. Some foods would be more difficult to eat. Getting dressed. The mundane things, that’s where his shame and guilt would lie. And gods, the *pity* that people would have on him. The way they’d look down at him and consider him lesser…

More than ever, somehow he knew he needed to prove himself. More than he ever needed to before, he needed to show *everyone* that he is the Blood of the Dragon. *And a dragon missing a leg is still a dragon.*

He cast his golden eyes to the side of the room where a full length body mirror was - he could barely see his new form. More scales than before, covering the left side of his body where he had been torn asunder. No arm. His torso bare and this time his hair was a *mess* down to his shoulders, not even put in its normal lazy bun when he was meant to be casual. Just loose rivers of black and gold hanging to his shoulders and down his back. 

“Gods I gotta get out of this room.” It’s only been less than twenty-four hours. He rolled out of bed, and immediately walked toward the door — still dressed in his pants from the night before and his boots, his top was singed away. Hopefully he could slip out before anyone noticed to nag him about taking care of himself. Maybe a duel would make him feel better…

Of course that hope was almost definitely dashed, when he stepped out of the room and was immediately met with —

((Open for whomever wants to be waiting to scold him or just in the common area to catch him))

Caspian looked stunned when he saw Vornakir's door open and he saw his roommate on his feet. He knew he shouldn't be surprised, the three of them had all agreed that they should be prepared for escape attempts as Vornakir recovered but for him to be on his feet so quickly was hard to believe.

"Whoa..." Caspian moved in closer, not enough to cut him off but close enough to catch him should his legs give out. "Glad to see you're recuperating quickly. I thought you were resting, how're you feeling?" Concern flickered across Caspian's face but its not pity, if the tables were reversed he'd be just as restless.

*And there it is, caught at the door.* He thought to himself. 

He looked about as good as one could expect given what he'd just gone through. And it probably was a good thing he was there, because it seemed like he really shouldn't be moving about just yet. The fact that he made it to his feet and walked across the room at all was a feat of strength in of itself. 

"I'm feeling...alright enough. I don't like being cooped up for too long, and feeling useless." He admitted.

"Think you can make it to that chair? I won't step in unless you need it." Caspian offered, pointing at one of their comfortable cushioned seats in the common area. "I can understand feeling stir crazy, but I'm not sure stumbling out into the hallways of the dorm half dressed is a very dignified reappearance into the student body."

Sarcasm aside, he added, "The others seem to be out or resting? I'm not sure, but I believe at least for right now I owe you a chat from the other night when we helped Redd?"

His auric eyes settled on the target across the room in the common area. Aside from the general mass of fatigue that accrued from his brush with Death that made his movements sluggish and uncertain, there was also the small portion of his torso and his entire left arm missing that changed his weight distribution. It made walking feel so strange and right-heavy. But none the less he made every effort to take careful, calculated steps across the room - stopping now and then only to lean against the wall or miscellaneous furnishing in the commons area. 

“Yeah, I figured everyone would be resting so now would be the perfect time to slip out so I can regain some *semblance* of my pride. It’s bad enough I couldn’t do anything to help, but worse that I couldn’t even die right when it came to protecting Zašia.” Once he made it to the chair, a grueling several minutes later for Caspian who was witness to it, he settled his stare on his dorm mate. “You don’t owe me anything really, I just thought it prudent to know more about you than your name and that you like food given the circumstances.”

Caspian watched stoically, he nodded approvingly as he saw the small signs of Vornakir's adjustment. "That's unfortunate for you that I just finished a workout and was going to get a snack Scales." he tried out the nickname, it seemed apt. 

"You did keep her safe, if that's what you wanted then mission accomplished."  There is a bit of reprimand in his words. The words tumble out after that, "You should know though that she blames herself for all of this. Redd too. Just remember dying for someone is easy, living for them is harder." He moves to take a seat across from Vornakir when he sits down and lands with a hard thump. 

Caspian's expression softened after a moment, guilt evident after his speech. He hadn't realized that there was anger mixed in with those emotions that he had bottled up over Vornakir's sacrifice "Sorry, that was harsh. You're barely on your feet I shouldn't have said that. Your comment about not dying *right* just set me off a bit. I guess if you wanted to know about me, you just learned that I have a bad habit of speaking without thought."

There was a long pause as the nickname was rendered. He weighed it on his tongue, tasting the way it would roll off mentally if others had said it in case it had caught on. He closed his eyes as he settled into the chair fully, but gave a curt nod seeming to not be opposed to it. 

He fixed Caspian with his draconic stare, sizing the other man up considerably. "It wasn't about keeping her safe, it was about making sure she didn't die for coming out to try to help me in the first place." He amended, watching his dorm mate settle across from him while the tension suddenly flared. 

His voice was lower, likely not wanting to disturb the others if they were in fact resting in their rooms but every word was like silk from his lips. "I won't begrudge you for your emotions. You should know by now, mine are complex. I don't mean to disparage our allies or...give any impression I fault them for feeling their own guilt. But Zasia was going to feel guilty whether I died, lived or nothing happened regardless. I can't speak for Redd. The truth is the only fault lies in me."

He trailed his gaze sidelong to his missing arm with melancholy evident. "I can live with the sacrifice, and the consequence. But the pity? I loathe the idea of that hanging over my head."

With Vornakir's stance made clear, Caspian took a minute to process what was said. He returns the stare unflinchingly, he's a warrior trained and tested. No matter his training, the bags under his eyes still betrayed his exhaustion. "Scales, listen... I don't pity you. In fact you might have even got off pretty easy for what happened. Ask most folks on their deathbed if they'd give their arm for another day they'd take it in a heartbeat."

The young man runs his hand through his hair and rubs his chin. "You did the selfless thing. I respect that more than you might realize. But you did by some miracle manage to live through that. No matter how much you might protest, you should see that as an opportunity not a failure."

His chin was raised still, not denoting shame in himself - But his stance on the pity was clear and remained. Pity was something he didn't want. There was still *burning pride* in him despite what occurred and whatever he may say. It was clear he would do it again if he had the chance to do it over. 

"I don't see it as...a failure, not really. I think it's just getting to me, knowing when I go out there every reaction is going to go from 'You could be the next Cynthia' and 'You're an exceptional student and duelist and person' and whatever other praise that I never used to care about getting to 'Oh, its him...The poor guy that lost his arm. How is he going to become anything worth while?' No matter how hot my pride may burn, this will remain a badge of pity on my honor. And to prevent that from happening I *have* to prove how this won't change me." 

If the word 'pride' hadn't already been thrown around so much, the more Caspian spoke with Vornakir the more he'd see just how...for a lack of better turn of phrase, *Draconic* he really was. He had their pride, their namesake, their scales, their immense power. It wouldn't take a genius to realize what was bothering him then. He felt like he *didn't do enough* when they were down there and now he felt like going forward he may be a burden to those around him.

The door swung open with more force than intended — not dramatic, just careless, like whoever opened it had stopped thinking three spells ago.

Redd stepped inside, shoulders tense, breath shallow. His shirt clung to him, damp and scorched at the right hem, the sleeve half-singed near the elbow. His hair, usually deliberate in its mess, was plastered back with sweat and dust. He didn’t so much walk in as drag himself in, all the sharp edges of grace dulled by sheer exhaustion.

He muttered, “Should’ve let him knock me on my ass the fun way.” to no one in particular — only to realize, with a sudden blink, that he wasn’t alone.

His gaze found Vornakir in the chair. Then Caspian. 

"Huh.”
It wasn’t surprise on his face, not really. More the exhausted confusion of someone who’d walked into a conversation three emotional miles deep and hadn’t the strength to circle back. He took a step further in, then leaned a hand on the back of a chair to steady himself.

“Well. Looks like I’m late to the brooding hour.” A weak smirk tugged at the edge of his mouth, but it didn’t quite stick. His eyes lingered on Vornakir longer than he probably meant to. There was no joke in them. Just quiet, raw relief.

“You’re up.” His voice cracked a little. “That's good.”

Then, softer:
“I was gonna come check on you, but Zurr’ka nearly cooked me alive. So. You beat me to it.”

Caspian had opened his mouth and begun to respond, “If getting back in fighting shape is what helps you get past this then…” when the door slammed open. He tensed and his head swiveled immediately before relaxing when he saw Redd. “Wow… did you and Zurr’ka take a nice trip to a volcano together or something?”

He turned back to Vornakir, “What I was trying to say is that I understand where you’re coming from. If you want back in action I can help you get there. Just if you want to come back from this and prove to them you don’t need their pity from day one I can help you get there. I’m sure you know this but it’s gonna hurt… a lot.” Hoping to avoid more contention he offers his assistance as an olive branch. It’s not just for show, he wants to see Vornakir back in his feet and this is the best way he knows how.

He opened his mouth to speak before the door abruptly opening cut him short and his eyes flashed to the door. He moved as if he were going to raise his left hand to ready some form of defense - instinct kicking in considering the wounded animal state he was in. But aside from realizing it wasn’t a threat, merely Redd looking like he tried to ride a dragon and barely made it out alive he also deflated as it dawned on him that when someone’s easier to strike with his left hand he still needed to use his right. 

For a long moment of silence from Vornakir, it became apparent that it was going to weigh heavily on him for some time. But he stowed that emotion and met Redd’s expression first. He carefully composed his best emotionally walled off form and gave a curt nod. “I don’t stay down long. I appreciate everyone pitching in to help, and being concerned with me though. You look a little rough yourself, is everything fine?”

But then he shifted his figure to the side some and fixed Caspian with a slightly less walled off gaze. “I’m aware it’ll hurt. But like you said, living for others is much more difficult than dying for them. I’m willing to endure. I refuse to be seen as lesser for doing something I know in my heart was the right thing just because I was near grievously wounded. So yes, I want to come back from this and show that whether I have both arms, or one, or neither, or blind or deaf or missing my damned legs. I will claw my way to greatness. I’m not sure how you intend to help me, but whatever is offered I won’t shy from.”

Caspian nods, a little bit of renewed vigor in the motion. "Great, I'll draw up a training regimen. Starting tomorrow we're gonna get your strength back up and then get you back on your feat and build up your endurance. You'll probably need some balance training based on what I saw earlier." 

He mumbles to himself about more training specifics, before continuing "I can even show you a few things if you want to practice with weapons. I'll expect you awake before first light tomorrow so we can get a full day in." He's not being harsh but the expectation is there, Vornakir asked for no pity so that's what he's getting.

Where others might find reason to protest or groan at the thought of waking up so early and dedicating time to a training regimen, especially a spell caster at that, Vornakir lifted his chin and pride swelled in his chest. “Balance training will be helpful, certainly.” He admitted. It’s important to acknowledge his strengths, sure, but to understand his weaknesses in this moment was crucial else it’s al for naught. 

Though considering his lack of a left arm, he glanced down to his open palm on his right hand that still sat on his…dirty pants. Yes this was just the day after all of that mess, and nobody likely wanted to undress him and bathe him after going through it themselves — that he would need to handle on his own later. But that reminder, the ash and dust and blood staining his breeches when he could do nothing but run…It sparked a fire in his chest.

“Yes. I believe it’s prudent to arm myself with a weapon in addition to my magic, at the very least as an option when I can’t rely on my magic. I’ve heard of Spellblades in my Circle back home whom dedicate their lives to magic *and* martial prowess and channeled their spells through blades as a focus rather than weaving signs and sigils. I believe that to be a worthy cause to dedicate time to. I will not disappoint.” He promised as he rested his eyes on Redd again while quietly wondering where Zašia was.

“Spellblade huh?” Caspian showed interest in the term but turned to Redd. “How about you? Seems like you might have your own coach at the moment?”

Caspian gets up and pours a few glasses of water for the three of them. He hands one to Redd, “Take a seat, we’re just sitting and chatting. I’ll admit our conversation has been a little intense but I think we’re due for a change of subject anyways.”

Redd accepted the glass from Caspian, fingers curling loosely around it. “Thanks,” he said, voice still a little rough from training. “And yeah—Zurr’ka’s been… thorough.” He took a sip, then glanced up with that faint, foxish grin. He caught himself, shaking his head. “Anyway. Point is, I’m learning what it’s like to actually sweat for something that isn’t a bad decision.”

His gaze slid to Vornakir, catching on the word Spellblade. “You know, I’ve been known to swing a Flameblade or two. Could be interesting. If you’re serious, we don’t have to wait. The obstacle garden’s open right now, easier footing than the dueling pit, and you can get used to your balance without some overeager first year taking your head off. Me and Zurr’ka could give you a few pointers. Caspian can play coach. Make it a double date.”

He crinkled his nose and looked between the pair. Then down at himself, "In truth I'd like to wait, minimum, til the morning. So I can at least get myself cleaned up and not reek of death, ash and blood nor look like a victim." He noted, as he was still mostly dressed how he was during the attack and not bathed given this was mere hours following it. 

"Give me the night to rest, recover a little, and gather myself. And at first light I'll meet whomever wants to help and we can get to work." He offered, giving himself a wobbly stand and found his balance, his eyes tracing down the hall toward his room. "What do you anticipate we tell Zurr'ka about all this since we haven't had a chance to churn our own fabricated story?"

Redd froze mid-step, the grin faltering before it could settle. His eyes flicked away, finding some unremarkable patch of wall to look at instead.

“…About that.” The words came low, like he had to force them past his teeth. “I might’ve… already told him. Everything.”

He shifted his weight, thumb running along the rim of his glass, restless. “It wasn’t—” He stopped, exhaled. “It wasn’t planned. He asked, and I— I didn’t want to give him some half-truth. Not about everything that's happened."

The guilt sat plain in the downturn of his mouth, in the way he couldn’t quite meet Vornakir’s eyes. “If you’re pissed, I get it. I should’ve asked you first. But I just…” He trailed off, shoulders lifting slightly, helpless. “Didn’t want to carry it alone.”

His voice dropped further, almost hesitant. “I usually push people away when things get ugly. Zurr’ka’s… the first person I’ve actually tried letting in. Figured maybe I’d start now, before I talk myself out of it.”

Redd’s words caught Caspian mid sip of water. He immediately choked and sputtered as the unanticipated answer hung in the air. “You…” he coughed a few times “…what?”

He looked to Vornakir and back to Redd trying to gauge the mood of the room.

Vornakir was finally standing, ready to retreat to his room to sleep so they could begin the next day of training - he was eager to begin so he wouldn’t be down and out for too long. However no sooner than he stood Redd would have unloaded his truth.

For some time he just stared. Then his head tilted back and let the Drow stare at the ceiling, his lips parted enough to know he was steadily rehearsing a reply. When he did speak, he craned his neck back forward with an audible crack of soreness and stiffness, his ochre eyes settling on the nervous man. “*Where did you tell him all of it at, exactly?*”

Redd’s jaw tightened, and for a moment it looked like he might sidestep the question. Then he let out a slow breath.

“The picnic on the hill,” he said quietly. “The one you two helped set up.” His thumb worried the rim of his glass. “Which… yeah, probably makes it worse. You went through all that to make it a good night, and I go and unload everything on him.”

His gaze dropped to the floor. “He just asked how I was, and I—” He shook his head. Eyes dropping to the floor. “I didn’t mean to. I just… broke. All of it came out. Not the kind of thing you drop on someone during a date.”

He glanced up at Vornakir, then away again. “I know it wasn’t just my secret to tell. But if I’d kept holding it in, I think I’d have cracked in half right there.”

A grimace spread across Caspian’s face. He knew Redd’s intentions were good but the reality of the circle of secrets adding another member frightened him. “Redd please be very specific, when you say you told him everything do you really mean everything?” His voice was shaky his head starting to spin from the implications.

He got up and began to pace a little. Eventually his eyes locked on Redd’s, “I hope your trust in Zurr’ka isn’t misplaced. It seems we don’t have a choice in the matter at this point.” 

Caspian slumps back into his seat, resolve and confidence faded for now, replaced by a resigned sense of dread… “Please tell me you at least made sure Velvyn wasn’t spying before you said anything.”

Redd didn’t flinch at Caspian’s tone, but his grip on the glass tightened until his knuckles paled.

“Yeah,” he said, voice low. “When I say everything, I mean everything. No half-truths. All of it.”

He watched Caspian pace, the unease on his face matching the weight in his gut. “I know what that means. I know what I’ve risked. But Zurr’ka’s not Velvyn. He won't twist what you give him. That’s not who he is. He won’t use this to hurt us. Not now, not ever” There was conviction there, but it wasn’t loud; more like he was trying to convince himself as much as them.

At the last question, he hesitated. “We were alone on the hill. No shadows, no stray footsteps that I noticed. I’m pretty sure if Velvyn had been there, I’d have felt it.” His brow furrowed slightly. “But I can’t promise she isn’t getting better at hiding.”

He looked between them, slower now. “You’re right there’s no undoing it. And if I’m wrong about him, that’s on me.

He pinched the bridge of his nose as he made his way down the hall. "I can attest that if a Drow wished to not be seen,, especially at night and one as tenacious as she is...she would not have been seen nor felt. And that's also not accounting for her access into Headwatch." 

With an arm out against the wall, he approached the door to his room and paused, looking around the corner. "I don't...usually have to say shit like this or...act like this..." He murmured, rubbing at the back of his neck with his remaining hand. 

"Thanks...For you know, helping save me. Not letting me die, and watching over me. And eventually training me to use shit I don't normally use. When I wake up tomorrow though, don't be surprised when I'm back to my normal self." He added with a cocksure grin at the pair. He settled his eyes on Redd a little longer, a furrow of his brow apparent. "There's no taking back what you have said or done, like you said. We just have to deal with fallout. I would just implore you ensure he promises his silence and compliance."

Then he flicked ochre eyes to Caspian. "First light. I'll meet you at the field, I might be a bit delayed - I'll stop by somewhere to get a sparring sword." With that, he opened his door and retreated into his room with the immediate latch of his lock clicking into place so he could shower and get dressed. 

((Feel free to continue a few more posts if you want, whenever you guys are ready we can move it to the next day for training RP, just ping me when its time for that and Vornakir will be there for the next phase of this scene - New thread or continue here!))

Redd stayed silent until the latch clicked and Vornakir’s footsteps faded. His hand lingered on the rim of his glass, thumb tracing the edge in slow circles.

“I didn’t…” His voice was low, uneven. “I didn’t really save him. Not like you did. I didn’t throw myself in front of anything or drag him back from the brink. I just. kept yelling and breaking things. And now he’s thanking me like I, like I did something that mattered.”

He exhaled, the sound heavy. “For what it’s worth, I did get his word. Zurr’ka promised he wouldn’t breathe a word to anyone, and I believe him. ((<@160196380323741696> I am making up the promise thing. Let me know if that's fine, but if not, we can remove it)) But still—” His mouth pressed into a thin line. “Doesn’t change the fact that I might’ve made all our lives life harder without asking. All because I couldn’t keep my damn mouth shut.”

A humorless laugh slipped out. “Truth is, I’m not sure I belong in that list of people who saved him. Caspian, you were the one who kept him breathing. You’ll be the one getting him back on his feet. Zasia stayed with him while we were taking care of the node. I just shouted from the sidelines."

Caspian having settled down a bit and been surprised by Vornakir’s sincerity before his retreat was quiet for a moment while Redd spoke. 

“Redd if you trust Zurr’ka then I’ll trust your decision. I’m just trying to process all this new information. Don’t beat yourself up for trusting him or for saying anything, you’ve been through a lot and we all could all probably use someone to talk about this stuff that’s not each other.” He rubs his eyes trying to stave off the exhaustion as if by force of will at this point. He’s only had a handful of hours of sleep and it’s showing. 

“When it comes to Vornakir you did plenty. We all kept him in one piece before we got to Aurum and that’s what matters, not who did what or how much. It took all four of us to fight that beholder, if anyone hadn’t been there it would’ve been worse.” Caspian stands up and starts to move towards his own room. He stops and puts a hand on Redd’s shoulder.

“You’re more than welcome to join us tomorrow, just don’t forget to take care of yourself. There’s more than just physical wounds to take care of in times like these.”

Redd didn’t answer right away, just let the weight of Caspian’s hand anchor him in place. His jaw worked once before he finally spoke.

“Yeah… maybe I will join you tomorrow. Could drag Zurr’ka along, make him work with more than just me for a change.” His mouth twitched like he wanted to smirk, but it didn’t quite get there.

“I just—” he blew out a slow breath, gaze dropping to the floor. “It’s easier to count the things I didn’t do than the ones I did. But… I hear you.”

Caspian’s reassurance hung in the air as he moved toward his room, and Redd gave a small nod in return. “Thanks, Cas. Really.”

Left alone in the common room, Redd sat back, the silence pressing in. *Did plenty. More than welcome.* Caspian had said it like Redd hadn’t spent the whole fight on the edge of being useless.

He rubbed a hand over his face, unsure if he was more exhausted from Zurr’ka’s training or from trying to believe a version of himself he couldn’t see.
